evals.prompt: I need a LinkedIn DM to Sarah Chen, VP of Revenue Operations at Datawise Analytics (Series B, 180 employees, sells a BI platform to mid-market finance teams). She posted on LinkedIn two days ago about her team cutting their Salesforce-to-NetSuite sync errors by 70% after rebuilding their integration layer in-house. My company is RevStack -- we sell a rev-ops automation platform. I'm Jamie Torres, Senior AE. The hook is her LinkedIn post about the integration rebuild. My ask should be a 15-minute call. Keep it tight for LinkedIn -- under 100 words.
evals.expected_output: A LinkedIn DM of 100 words or fewer that opens with a reference to Sarah Chen's specific LinkedIn post about cutting Salesforce-to-NetSuite sync errors by 70% through an in-house integration rebuild. The first sentence is about her achievement, not about RevStack or Jamie. The message bridges from her integration challenge to how RevStack works with rev-ops teams in similar situations, then closes with a single ask: a 15-minute call. Sign-off is Jamie Torres, Senior AE, RevStack. Followed by a Five Laws check section confirming all five laws pass (specific reference: the LinkedIn post about 70% sync error reduction; lead with them: first sentence references her team's result; one ask: 15-minute call; word count: under 100; banned language: none present). Notes section states the hook, tone (peer-level), and confidence (HIGH -- specific public post referenced). Then Day 3 follow-up (different angle, e.g., reframe around what other rev-ops teams are doing post-integration-rebuild, sent via email since primary was LinkedIn) and Day 7 follow-up (shorter, single question, e.g., 'Is cleaning up the data layer after your integration rebuild even a priority right now?'). Ends with ALL OUTREACH OUTPUTS REQUIRE HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE SENDING.
expectations: Message opens with a direct reference to Sarah Chen's LinkedIn post about cutting Salesforce-to-NetSuite sync errors by 70% (drives reply rate) — the first sentence must name her specific achievement, not RevStack or Jamie, because prospect-first openers outperform sender-first openers in cold outreach response rates

Message body is 100 words or fewer and the single CTA is a 15-minute call with no alternative asks or vague 'let me know' language (drives deal velocity) — a single specific ask with a defined time commitment converts at higher rates than multi-option or open-ended closes

Message avoids corporate jargon (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, solution, game-changing, revolutionary, disruptive, holistic, end-to-end, cutting-edge, world-class, innovative, empower, unlock, transform) — messages with these words have measurably lower reply rates in B2B cold outreach

Output includes a Five Laws compliance audit section verifying all 5 laws (specific reference, lead with them, one ask, word count, banned language) — this structured self-check reduces rep review time and catches messages that would get ignored before they're sent

Output includes a Day 3 follow-up on a different angle (not 'just checking in') and via a different channel since primary was LinkedIn, plus a Day 7 follow-up in single-question format (drives deal velocity) — a pre-built multi-touch sequence saves the rep 15-20 minutes of drafting and keeps the cadence moving without manual effort

Notes section includes the specific hook used, tone classification, and a confidence rating tied to verifiable signal quality (drives pipeline hygiene) — this metadata lets a sales manager audit outreach quality at scale and ensures reps only send messages backed by real prospect signals

evals.prompt: Draft a cold email for me. Prospect is Marcus Rivera, Director of Sales Enablement at TerraForm Logistics (1,200 employees, $180M revenue, freight and logistics). His company just announced a partnership with Maersk to handle North American intermodal freight -- it was in FreightWaves last Thursday. They're scaling their sales team from 40 to 70 reps this quarter according to their careers page. I sell Quota OS, a sales capacity planning tool. I'm Priya Nair, Account Executive at Quota OS. I want to ask for a 20-minute demo. Email channel, keep it under 150 words.
evals.expected_output: A cold email of 150 words or fewer with a subject line that is specific to TerraForm Logistics's situation (e.g., referencing scaling the sales team or the Maersk partnership -- not generic like 'Quick question' or 'Introduction'). The email opens with a reference to the FreightWaves announcement about the Maersk partnership and/or the 40-to-70 rep hiring ramp visible on their careers page. First sentence is about TerraForm's expansion, not about Quota OS or Priya. The body bridges from their scaling challenge (nearly doubling the sales team) to how Quota OS helps companies plan capacity during rapid hiring. Closes with one ask: a 20-minute demo. Sign-off: Priya Nair | Account Executive | Quota OS. Followed by a Five Laws check section confirming all five laws pass (specific references: FreightWaves Maersk announcement and careers page headcount data; lead with them: first sentence about their expansion; one ask: 20-minute demo; word count: under 150; banned language: confirmed clean). Notes section with hook description, tone, and confidence level. Then Day 3 follow-up (different angle -- e.g., reframe around the quota-setting challenge when onboarding 30 new reps, not 'just following up') and Day 7 follow-up (shorter, single question, e.g., 'Is capacity planning even the bottleneck as you scale to 70 reps, or is it something else entirely?'). Ends with ALL OUTREACH OUTPUTS REQUIRE HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE SENDING.
expectations: Subject line references TerraForm Logistics's specific situation — the Maersk partnership, the 40-to-70 rep scale, or sales capacity during expansion (drives reply rate) — prospect-specific subject lines outperform generic ones ('Quick question', 'Introduction') by 2-3x on open rate in cold email

First sentence references the FreightWaves Maersk announcement or the careers page hiring ramp, not Quota OS or Priya (drives reply rate) — opening with a verifiable, timely event the prospect cares about signals research effort and earns the read past the first line

Email body is 150 words or fewer with exactly one CTA — a 20-minute demo — and no secondary asks, no 'or if you prefer a call', no vague 'thoughts?' (drives deal velocity) — single-CTA cold emails have significantly higher conversion to booked meetings than multi-option emails

Message avoids corporate jargon (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, solution, game-changing, revolutionary, disruptive, holistic, end-to-end, cutting-edge, world-class, innovative, empower, unlock, transform) — messages with these words have measurably lower reply rates in B2B cold outreach

Output includes a Five Laws compliance audit section verifying all 5 laws (specific reference, lead with them, one ask, word count, banned language) — this structured self-check reduces rep review time and catches messages that would get ignored before they're sent

Output includes a Day 3 follow-up using a different angle with new value (e.g., quota-setting challenges when onboarding 30 reps) and a Day 7 follow-up in single-question format (drives deal velocity) — a pre-built 3-touch sequence eliminates the rep needing to draft follow-ups from scratch, keeping cadence momentum without manual effort

Notes section includes the specific hooks used, a confidence rating tied to source quality (FreightWaves = published media, careers page = publicly verifiable), and tone classification (drives pipeline hygiene) — this metadata lets managers audit whether reps are sending outreach backed by real signals or fabricated personalization

evals.prompt:
Here's my research brief on the prospect. Use it to draft a cold email.

---
RESEARCH BRIEF -- Elena Vasquez / CloudNine Health

Prospect: Elena Vasquez, VP of Customer Success at CloudNine Health
Company: CloudNine Health -- cloud EHR platform for mid-size hospital systems, 450 employees, Series D ($90M raise in Jan 2026 led by Andreessen Horowitz)
Hook: CloudNine Health's Q4 2025 NPS dropped from 62 to 47 according to their G2 reviews trend (publicly visible). Elena posted on LinkedIn in February about 'rethinking how we onboard hospital IT teams' after a contract loss at Mercy Regional. She also spoke at a HIMSS side event on reducing time-to-value for health IT implementations.
Timing: Post-Series-D, likely under pressure to show net revenue retention to justify the raise. NPS drop and contract loss suggest churn risk is top of mind.
ICP match: STRONG -- mid-market health tech, CS leader, expansion-stage.
---

I sell OnboardIQ, a customer onboarding orchestration platform for B2B SaaS. I'm Daniel Park, AE at OnboardIQ. Ask should be whether it's worth a 15-minute conversation. Email channel, 150 words max.
evals.expected_output: A cold email of 150 words or fewer with a subject line referencing CloudNine Health's onboarding challenge or NPS situation (not generic). The email opens with a reference to Elena Vasquez's LinkedIn post about rethinking hospital IT team onboarding or the publicly visible G2 NPS trend dropping from 62 to 47. First sentence is about CloudNine Health's situation, not about OnboardIQ or Daniel. The body connects the onboarding challenge (which Elena herself flagged publicly) to how OnboardIQ works with health tech CS teams facing similar time-to-value pressure. Closes with one ask: whether a 15-minute conversation is worth it. Sign-off: Daniel Park | AE | OnboardIQ. Followed by a Five Laws check section confirming all five laws pass (specific references: Elena's LinkedIn post on rethinking onboarding, G2 NPS trend, and/or HIMSS side event talk; lead with them: first sentence about CloudNine Health's onboarding challenge; one ask: 15-minute conversation; word count: under 150; banned language: confirmed clean). Notes section with hook used (research brief signals: NPS drop + LinkedIn post + HIMSS talk), tone (peer-level or educational), and confidence (HIGH -- multiple verifiable signals from research brief). Then Day 3 follow-up (different angle -- e.g., reference the HIMSS talk on time-to-value instead of the NPS data, or share a relevant data point about onboarding timelines in health tech) and Day 7 follow-up (shorter, single question, e.g., 'Is onboarding time-to-value the thing keeping you up at night post-Series-D, or is it something else?'). Does NOT reference the Mercy Regional contract loss directly (could feel invasive -- the research brief flags it but the LinkedIn post is the safe public signal). Ends with ALL OUTREACH OUTPUTS REQUIRE HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE SENDING.
expectations: First sentence references Elena Vasquez's LinkedIn post about rethinking hospital IT onboarding or the publicly visible G2 NPS drop from 62 to 47, not OnboardIQ or Daniel (drives reply rate) — opening with the prospect's own publicly stated challenge creates immediate relevance and signals the rep did real research, not a mail merge

Message uses only publicly safe signals from the research brief (LinkedIn post, G2 reviews trend, HIMSS talk) and does NOT reference the Mercy Regional contract loss directly (compliance/risk) — referencing a private contract loss would feel invasive and could damage the brand's reputation, even if the data came from a research brief

Email body is 150 words or fewer with exactly one CTA — whether a 15-minute conversation is worth it — framed as low-commitment and easy to say yes to (drives deal velocity) — soft-ask framing ('is it worth a conversation') converts better on high-seniority prospects like VPs than hard-ask framing ('let me show you a demo')

Message avoids corporate jargon (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, solution, game-changing, revolutionary, disruptive, holistic, end-to-end, cutting-edge, world-class, innovative, empower, unlock, transform) — messages with these words have measurably lower reply rates in B2B cold outreach

Output includes a Five Laws compliance audit section verifying all 5 laws (specific reference, lead with them, one ask, word count, banned language) — this structured self-check reduces rep review time and catches messages that would get ignored before they're sent

Day 3 follow-up uses a different signal from the research brief than the primary message (e.g., HIMSS talk on time-to-value if the primary used the NPS data) and Day 7 follow-up is in single-question format (drives deal velocity) — rotating signals across touches prevents the sequence from feeling repetitive and gives the prospect a new reason to engage on each touch

Notes section includes confidence rating tied to the number and quality of verifiable signals (LinkedIn post + G2 data + HIMSS talk = multiple independent public sources) and explicitly flags which signals were used vs. held back (drives pipeline hygiene) — this audit trail lets managers verify that outreach personalization is backed by real data, not hallucinated
